coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from agro waste biomass for pioneering bioanalysis and in vivo bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Manjubaashini, N.
Bargavi, P.
Balakumar, S.
coredata.dc:description: Waste to Wealth’s mission aims to develop valuable products for choice of environmental and biological purposes, especially from agricultural wastes. A vast variety of organic and inorganic elements that are left over from agricultural activities like crop cultivation or livestock raising are referred to as agricultural waste, for example, pista shell (PS) and groundnut shell (GNS). These agricultural waste materials can be effectively utilized for preparing wealth materials depending on materials and their composition. Owing to the presence of high carbon content in these waste materials, preparing carbon-based nanostructured wealth materials for environmental and biological applications is significant, interestingly, it also fulfills the sustainable approach along with a cost-effective strategy. This hypothesis led us to prepare one such type of carbon-based nanostructured material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), from PS and GNS agro-wastes. These agro-wastes derived CQDs were formulated by hydrothermal method and their distinct structural, morphological, functional, optical, and bioactive properties were determined. The XRD data divulges the amorphous nature of CQDs with interlayer spacing (∼0.30 nm) similar to graphite. HRTEM images express spherical morphology with a particle size of ca. 5 nm and ca. 12 nm for PSE and GNSE CQDs, respectively. Raman spectra divulge a doublet D band (1335 and 1447 cm−1) with ID/IG ratio of 1.6 and 1.2 for PSE and GNS CQDs, respectively. Using UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy data reveals that PSE and GNS CQDs show excitation at 370 and 309 nm, and emission at 456 and 440 nm, respectively. Further, high fluorescence quantum yield (9.5 % for PSE and 9.0 % for GNS), virtuous cell viability (90 % for both CQDs), high hemocompatibility (0.3 for PSE and 0.1 % for GNS), accelerated anti-bacterial activity, protein denaturation, bacteria interaction, and growth factors (IGF-1, Col-1) expression levels enable these CQDs to function as a probe for live quantitative bioimaging in Zebrafish.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Bioimaging
Fluorescence
Biocompatibility
Hydrothermal

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Government of India Department of Biotechnology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: NM and SB acknowledge the department of biotechnology, Government of India, for Research Associate fellowship, MHRD-RUSA [C3/RI&QI/RUSA2.0/Theme/Group-2/Award/2021/034, dt 03/02/ 2021] for funding and DST-FIST Funded X-Ray Diffractometer Facility, National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras [ SR/FST/ET-1/2020/694 (C); dt 23 rd June 2021].

NM and SB acknowledge the department of biotechnology, Government of India, for Research Associate fellowship, MHRD-RUSA [C3/RI&QI/RUSA2.0/Theme/Group-2/Award/2021/034, dt 03/02/ 2021] for funding and DST-FIST Funded X-Ray Diffractometer Facility, National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Guindy campus, Chennai [SR/FST/ET-1/2020/694 (C); dt 23rd June 2021].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of (a) PSE, (b) GNSE CQDs.
HRTEM micrographs of (a) PSE, (b) GNSE CQDs.
Raman spectra of (a) PSE, (b) GNSE CQDs.
XPS spectra of (a) PSE and (b) GNSE CQDs.

(a-b) Excitation spectra of PSE and GNSE CQDs, (c-d) Emission spectra of PSE and GNSE CQDs, (e-i and f-i) Deconvoluted PL spectra of PSE and GNSE CQDs, (e-ii and f-ii) Energy level diagram of PSE and GNSE CQDs, (g-h) contour mapping of PSE and GNSE CQDs.
Cell viability values (%) estimated by direct MTT assay of CQDs (5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 µL) against fibroblast cells.
Percentage of hemolysis for various concentrations of PSE and GNSE CQDs.
Anti-inflammatory activity of PSE and GNSE CQDs by protein inhibition.
Study of relative gene expression using IGF-1 and Col-1 primers treated with GNSE and PSE CQDs.
Bright-field and fluorescence imaging of zebrafish; (a) PSE and (b) GNSE CQDs. The images were captured at a 400 μm scale bar.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from agro waste biomass for pioneering bioanalysis and in vivo bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Chennai 600025, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Chennai 600025, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Oral Pathology, Saveetha Dental College and Hospitals, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences, Chennai 600077, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Oral Pathology, Saveetha Dental College and Hospitals, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences, Chennai 600077, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Waste to Wealth’s mission aims to develop valuable products for choice of environmental and biological purposes, especially from agricultural wastes. A vast variety of organic and inorganic elements that are left over from agricultural activities like crop cultivation or livestock raising are referred to as agricultural waste, for example, pista shell (PS) and groundnut shell (GNS). These agricultural waste materials can be effectively utilized for preparing wealth materials depending on materials and their composition. Owing to the presence of high carbon content in these waste materials, preparing carbon-based nanostructured wealth materials for environmental and biological applications is significant, interestingly, it also fulfills the sustainable approach along with a cost-effective strategy. This hypothesis led us to prepare one such type of carbon-based nanostructured material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), from PS and GNS agro-wastes. These agro-wastes derived CQDs were formulated by hydrothermal method and their distinct structural, morphological, functional, optical, and bioactive properties were determined. The XRD data divulges the amorphous nature of CQDs with interlayer spacing (∼0.30 nm) similar to graphite. HRTEM images express spherical morphology with a particle size of ca. 5 nm and ca. 12 nm for PSE and GNSE CQDs, respectively. Raman spectra divulge a doublet D band (1335 and 1447 cm−1) with ID/IG ratio of 1.6 and 1.2 for PSE and GNS CQDs, respectively. Using UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy data reveals that PSE and GNS CQDs show excitation at 370 and 309 nm, and emission at 456 and 440 nm, respectively. Further, high fluorescence quantum yield (9.5 % for PSE and 9.0 % for GNS), virtuous cell viability (90 % for both CQDs), high hemocompatibility (0.3 for PSE and 0.1 % for GNS), accelerated anti-bacterial activity, protein denaturation, bacteria interaction, and growth factors (IGF-1, Col-1) expression levels enable these CQDs to function as a probe for live quantitative bioimaging in Zebrafish.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For the reason of high sensitivity, excellent spatial, and temporal resolution, practical non-invasive design, and capacity for real-time monitoring, fluorescence, or bioimaging, has gained prominence in recent years in the fields of biological research, clinical diagnosis, live imaging, wound tracking, and therapeutics applications [1]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) reveal a size lesser than 10 nm and exceptional physicochemical properties, such as non-toxic, inexpensive, chemically inert, wide absorption and emission wavelengths, exceptional fluorescence properties, easily produced, high-water solubility, environmentally friendly, biocompatible, and high photostability [2]. The sp2-conjugated structure of CQDs is encircled by oxygen and functional groups like carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. These characteristics make CQDs excellent fluorescent candidates for wide applications such as drug administration, photocatalysis, pharmaceutical analysis, biosensing, and bioimaging [3].

The existing literatures on CQDs preparation were mostly expensive and derived from chemical precursors that are hazardous to the environment. Despite the aforementioned rationale, inexpensive and sustainable carbon sources are frequently used for the production of CQDs. The synthesis of CQDs from sustainable, environmentally friendly feedstocks particularly agro-waste biomass is a successful approach that offers the twin benefits of waste management and advanced material synthesis [4]. In recent years, the synthesis of CQDs has come to rely more and more on the biomass source from agro-waste [5,6]. It has a variety of organic substrates, including proteins, carbs, carotenoids, as well as alkaloids. With differing degrees of success, nearly all agro biomass wastes, including fruits, vegetables, wheat wastes, corn surplus, textile lint waste, recycled papers, dry fruit shells, and frequently observed plants, have been tested as carbon precursors [7]. Biomass from agricultural waste is a consistent and sustainable source of raw materials that has the potential to be a viable substitute feedstock for the production of CQDs [8]. The left-over plant materials after crop harvesting are meant as agro wastes including more cellulose (50 %), hemicelluloses (35 %), and lignin (25 %). Owing to the affordability, substantial quantity, and extensive range, the lignocellulosic contents are regarded as the utmost auspicious source of carbon for microbiological activities [9].

With their abundance of accessibility, cost-effectiveness, and minimal processing requirements, it is reasonable to assume that agricultural wastes and byproducts, like cereal straws, are low-cost adsorbents [10,11]. Thus, the materials possess more physicochemical properties, effective manipulation, readiness in large quantities, and a defined waste-to-wealth system [12].

“Top-down” and “bottom-up” preparation techniques can be used to classify CQD preparation techniques based on the association between the carbon source and its significance. The sp2 carbon domains are renovated into reduced CQDs by the top-down approach, which mainly uses chemical oxidation, laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical synthesis techniques [13]. The bottom-up method produces carbon with sp2 domains by combining hydrothermal, microwave, and pyrolysis progressions. This enables CQDs to have controlled both morphology as well as size. The hydrothermal approach is a usually employed method in bottom-top techniques. Reaction yield is relatively high, and the process is simple to control and environmentally friendly [14]. The biomass (pista shell (PS) and groundnut shell (GNS)) waste-derived CQDs with low cytotoxicity are suitable for biomedical applications like bibliometric analysis, bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, and diagnosis applications [15–17]. The PS consists of triglycerides, cellulose (42 %), hemicellulose (15.2 %), and lignin (13.5 %) and their chemical composition shows carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen [18]. Whereas, GNS upholds 20–48 wt% cellulose, 1–3 wt% hemicellulose, and 10–28 wt% lignin. Interestingly, GNS has high porosity and water absorption capacity. In addition, chemical constituents like iron, alumina, silica, and calcium make its chemical structure [19].

The external defensive layer of PS is rich in bioactive constituents of polyphenols, unsaturated fatty acids, dietary fibers, essential oils, and tocopherols (vitamin E) [20]. As the research indicates these bioactive composites originate in PS was valued as good antioxidants. Exploiting the byproducts (waste material) from nutritious food products like PS deserves numerous applications. As discussed, here, CQDs were prepared from a useful material from the waste (PS) as it decomposes slowly (∼3 yrs) [21]. PS might be evidence as a treasured resource in the pitches of biosensing, diagnosis, drug delivery, imaging, and bacterial interaction assay.

Therefore, the key characteristics of the CQDs made from agro biomass waste are their affordability, high availability, therapeutic potential, and environmental friendliness [22]. In addition, PS and GNS components including heteroatoms, nitrogen, hydrogen, and other substances like proteins, carbohydrates, and amino acids that are appropriate for the production of quality CQDs and applications such as biosensing, targeted medication delivery, electronics, live cell imaging, tissue regeneration, in addition to therapies [23].

Here, this article reports CQDs made from agro biomass waste (PS and GNS) for practical biological applications. The PS and GNS-derived CQDs were synthesized via hydrothermal technique, valued all the physiochemical properties, and evolved them in vitro merits like cell viability, hemocompatibility, anti-bacterial activity, bacterial interaction, and gene expression. Moreover, in vivo imaging was conducted using Zebrafish as a model for testing CQDs uptake capability, and toxicity, and utilizing the CQDs as high efficiency to resemble the genome of human anatomy. CQD formation from agro biomass waste is the most distinctive feature of this study. The generated CQDs will be extremely bioactive, absorbent, biodegradable, and a chemically active compound for prolific biological examination. Rarely, the PS and GNS-derived CQDs combined for intricate live imaging applications (from cellular to organ level).

This article reports the preliminary novelty by designing CQDs from waste to wealth, and GNS and PS from innumerable farming biomass wastes. The GNS, which is the raw material used to form CQD, is easily accessible; and annually 900,000 tons of GNS biomass waste are procured every year [24]. One of the chief novelties and benefits of our synthesis process is that the carbon-based nanomaterial that was produced, CQDs, is air- and light-stable for an extended period, even longer than a year or so. This is due to the slow decomposition of the PS was accounted for. The progressions of cleaning, drying, crushing, extracting, and hydrothermal treating fashioned the aforementioned CQDs for imaging applications. As an upshot, the purification of CQDs finds innovative and beneficial applications from the slow degrading PS, repurposing waste in an environmentally friendly and sustainable manner as part of the waste administration policy.

PS and GNS were collected from the farmers of the corresponding agriculture fields. The CQDs powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) formation was detected by X-Pert PANalytical, Netherlands using the X-ray source as Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.542 Å). Higher resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) metaphors were held by (200 kV FE-TEM, model JEM-2100F) JEOL at CUSAT, Kochi, India. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements were investigated by (FTIR-8400S, SHIMADZU) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 as ATR mode. Raman vibrations were detected using WiTec, Germany with 532 nm laser. Excitation spectra were recorded using UV–Visible spectroscopy, LAB MAN, India. ELISA Plate Analyser by ROBONIK the supernatant is measured at 540 nm. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) using HORIBA, Fluoromax. Zeta potential analyser SZ-100 V2 series, Horiba, Japan. Fluorescence microscope imaging using EVOS Life Technology Spectro fluorophotometer at 298 K at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. Real-time PCR (RT-PCR) expressions using ABI, Applied Biosystems in Foster City, California at Saveetha dental college, Chennai, India. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy was recorded on Thermo Scientific (MULTILAB 2000 Base system with X-Ray, Auger and ISS) with Twin Anode Mg/Al (300/400 W) X-Ray Source at SRM University, Chennai, India.

The collected PS and GNS were cleaned, and washed thrice with water and ethanol. The well washed PS and GNS were weighed and placed in a 1000 mL glass beaker containing 800 mL of double distilled (DD) water and kept at 100 °C for boiling up to 3 h. The suspension was allowed to cool to room temperature (RT) following 3 h of heating. Next, 50 ml of groundnut shell extraction (GNSE) and pista shell extraction (PSE) were each poured into a different hydrothermal autoclave. Then, the autoclaves were sealed and sustained at 200 °C up to 12 h. While the mixture touched ambient temperature, it was washed using centrifugation. After centrifuging, the wabbly solution was filtered via a microporous (∼0.22 μM) membrane to eliminate insoluble precipitates. The obtained brown solutions of PSE and GNSE CQDs were gathered and kept at 4 °C for future analysis (Fig. 1 ).

Both PS and GNS were composed of several components like cellulose, lignin, etc., which possess anti-oxidant properties, cellular uptake, cell labeling, and fluorescence excitation from cell membranes [25]. Further, the PS shows natural exfoliants owing to its hydroxy group [26]. The GNS chemical composition like silica, alumina, and calcium will make them vital for tissue regeneration applications [27]. The presented PS and GNS CQDs will act as effective anti-bacterial agents [28] and for intercellular imaging by strong excitation-dependent emission characteristics [29]. The CQDs demonstrated efficient fluorescent tagging with excellent fluorescence ability [30]. CQDs derived from PS and GNS biomass have multiple emission states and band gaps, which makes them effective for imaging cells, inducing growth factors, and interacting with bacteria. Studies on bacterial interactions, anti-bacterial agents, and bioimaging are made possible by the manipulation of CQD emission spectra. The CQDs’ various emission spectra were utilized to identify and measure the reaction’s progress in real-time.

The obtained XRD pattern was analysed to categorize the crystallographic assets of PSE also GNSE CQDs with the formation of (002) plane as shown in Fig. 2 . The synthesized compound (PSE and GNSE CQDs) shows a distinctive broad-ranged diffraction peak of CQDs, which dispensed for decrepit carbon atoms at a high degree of 2θ at 24.3° and lattice spacing of (002) by demonstrating the occurrence of CQDs amorphous nature in the lattice spacing [31]. PSE and GNSE CQDs interlayer spacing is 0.33 and 0.32 nm respectively were calculated using Bragg’s law which is typically similar to the interlayer spacing (0.33 nm) of graphite. The CQD produced by hydrothermal treatment delivers low intense, broad spectra, and with reduced background noise.

Fig. 3 evidently confirms the spherical morphology of PSE and GNSE CQDs using HRTEM imaging as viewed in Fig. 2a and b. The PSE and GNSE CQDs particle size distribution were exposed in Fig. 2a(ii) and b(ii) inset. A regularly distributed and resembled uniform morphology with the particle size of 4.5–5 and 10–14 nm for PSE and GNSE CQDs respectively (Fig. 2a(ii) and 2b(iii)). Correspondingly, it is found that some aggregated particles as shown in Fig. 2a(i) and b(i) owing to the hygroscopically induced aggregation process [32]. The deprived crystalline formation of CQDs is owing to the formation of increased oxygen-rich groups, that further established the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CQDs that are revealed in Fig. 3a(i) and b(i).

The FTIR spectra of PSE CQDs show a broad band at 3334 cm−1 for O-H vibration, 2494 cm−1 for CC, 1634 cm−1 for CO, and HOH stretching, 1467 cm−1 revealed for C-C and 668 cm−1 displayed for C-H bending formations (Figure SI1a). As shown in Fig. SI1b, the GNSE CQD reveals peaks at 3311, 2359, 1783, 1629, and 826 cm−1 were elevated for O-H, CC, CO stretching, and CO bending vibrations respectively. Accordingly, the bands indicate the hydroxyl and carboxylic groups, and their vibrations were instigated by cellulose and lignin in the PS and GNS biomass [33]. The C-H stretching vibrations (668 cm−1) are associated with the elements of PS (carbon, nitrogen, oxygen), GNS (carbon, silica, calcium, nitrogen, oxygen), and their organic ligands. The –CO vibrational peaks at (1634 and 1629 cm−1) in CQDs determines the stretching vibrations of surface carboxyl or carbonyl groups. Further, the hydroxyl groups (–OH) in CQDs were noted as O-H stretching vibrations (3334 and 3311 cm−1).

Raman spectrum of CQDs exhibits G and D band peaks at 1333 and 1586 cm−1 for PSE and 1340 and 1597 cm−1 for GNSE CQDs respectively (Fig. 4 a-b). The defect band is related to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds of the disordered graphite. The graphene band is linked with the sp2 carbon atom as a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice [34]. The ID/IG ratios are 1.06 and 1.01 for PSE and GNSE CQD, respectively. This indicates that the CQD has a sizable number of structural flaws. The ID/IG value also shows that the structure of the CQDs contains an expanse of amorphous carbon that is consistent with XRD patterns. Thus, the higher intensity D bands demonstrate the existence of a significant amount of carbon. Further, in the Raman spectra of GNSE CQD the peak at 1106 cm−1 represents Si-O stretching vibration. The presence of silica in GNS may introduce additional peaks in the Raman spectrum. GNS contains 42 wt% of Si from the total elemental compositions. Thus, the CQDs Si content was revealed at 1106 cm−1 as Si-O stretching vibration.

XPS offers comprehensive insights, encompassing the chemical composition, surface characteristics, and electronic structure of the CQDs. The XPS survey spectra of PSE and GNSE CQDs are depicted in Fig. 5 a(i) and b(i) along with their atomic percentage. The peaks at ∼284, ∼400 and ∼531 eV was ascribed to C1s, O1s, and N1s respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C1s were decomposed into three distinct peaks for PSE and GNSE at binding energies of ∼284.6 eV for C–C bonds, 283.22 for C-O bonds, 285.82 eV for C-N bonds, and ∼288.22, 286.14 eV for OCO and COC bonds [35], indicative of various carbon species as shown in Fig. 5a(ii) and b(ii). The high-resolution N1s spectra, shown in Fig. 5a(iii) and b(iii), featured peaks at binding energies of ∼398.4, ∼400.3, and 399.67 eV. These peaks are attributed to the Si3N4, C-NH2, and NSi2O configuration states indicating the presence of N in three different forms, respectively [36]. Similarly, the O1s XPS spectrum revealed the major peaks at binding energies of 529.98, 531.20, 532.99 eV, and 533.2 eV, corresponding to oxygen in CO, COH, NCO, and CO (or NO) states [37], respectively, as depicted in Fig. 5 a(iv) and b(iv) respectively.

The absorption curve of PSE and GNSE CQDs is labelled in Fig. 6 a-b. From the spectrum, the cutoff wavelengths of CQDs were found at 240 and 242 nm for PSE and GNSE CQD respectively. The absorption of CQDs at 240 and 242 nm cause π-π* transition in aromatic CC bonds. Moreover, the PSE and GNSE CQDs showed broad peaks at 370 and 309 nm, which were linked to the n-π* transition of surface functional groups such as CO [38,39]. Furthermore, the 370 and 309 nm peaks certainly indicate CQDs fluorescence by tuning the excitation energy, and that enhances the emission energy.

The emission bands of PSE and GNSE CQDs are exhibited in Fig. 6c-d. The normalized emission peaks at 456 and 440 nm were assigned for PSE and GNSE CQDs respectively. The emission peaks revealed from the excitation spectrum for PSE and GNSE CQDs were observed as 240 and 242 nm. The PSE CQD emits bright blue light at 365 nm in the UV lamp which mentions the increased quantum yield of about 9.5 %. Meanwhile, contrarily, the GNSE CQD emits bright green light at 365 nm in the UV lamp which refers to the quantum yield of 9.0 % (Fig. 6c-d insert).

Deconvolution of PL spectra of CQDs involves separating the spectrum into its constituent peaks to better understand the emission features and the underlying electronic transitions. The deconvoluted PL peaks of PSE CQDs express violet (434 nm), blue (459 nm), cyan (497 nm), and green (545 nm) emissions at 2.58, 2.70, 2.49, and 2.27 eV respectively, which corresponds for the blue shift in the emission wavelength (Fig. 6e(i)). This blue shift may be associated with a decrease in the graphitic-N content or other structural changes like C sp2 domains, oxygen states, CO and CN bonds [40]. The deconvoluted emission spectra of GNSE CQDs reveal violet emission at 443 and 406 nm (2.79 and 3.05 eV) owing to the scattered incident light, and blue emission at 493 nm (2.5 eV) (Fig. 6f(i)). Violet light, with shorter wavelengths and higher energy compared to blue light, is observed. The progression of emissions is due to nitrogen and sulfur in the CQDs [41]. The transition of electrons from the valence band and the nearby conduction band is responsible for the excitation-dependent luminescence (6e(ii) and 6f(ii)). Different energy levels may be introduced in the energy gap positions by the abundant surface groups of CQD. Different emissions arise from these multiple and distinct electronic transition modes [42].

Ex at 370 nm; Em of 456 nm and Ex of 309 nm; Em of 440 nm for PSE and GNSE, respectively, were used to estimate the PL contour pattern of CQDs. Fig. 6g-h illustrates a broad and incessant range of contour mapping for the CQDs with visual emission distinguishment. Higher intensity emission in the brighter area is ascribed to reduced particle dimension. The mapping with distraction spectra was dispensed by the non-uniformed particle dimension and inconstant range of excitations (240 and 370 nm, Fig. 6a-b). The higher energy is represented by red, and the lower energies are signified as orange, yellow, green, and blue. The surface passivation results from oxygen functional groups, which successfully trap the excitons and prevent the non-radiative recombination, which is associated with the PL spectra. Fluorescence with a large spectral reveal’s discrete energy levels in CQDs. The increased excitation wavelength was observed in the energy band gap as a result of surface states. 2D spectral excitation and emission medium of PSE CQD reveals a narrow excitation at 360–390 nm and a broad emission region spanning 440–490 nm, offering the potential for the formation of blue light. Conversely, the GNSE CQD exhibits a wide spread in both emission (400–490 nm) and excitation (290–350 nm), indicating its capability for green light emission. The PSE CQDs with extended visible excitation spectra pay the way for the absorption of CQDs into the cell nucleus. In the case of GNSE CQDs, the UV range excitation spectra passivate CQDs for labeling up to cell membrane and cytoplasm. Thus, the CQDs derived from different biomass with varying fluorescence ranges will be adopted for more accurate imaging [43]. In biological imaging of the zebrafish model at various wavelengths, the broad range of stronger and brighter PL contour spectrum (Fig. 6g-h) and discrete values of CQDs fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 6e-f) demonstrate relevance. For in vivo imaging, the fluorescence analysis of CQDs thus provides a means of determining their presence in the zebrafish model. When zebrafish are imaged, the fluorescence property provides improved photostability for extended period of time (24 h) of fluorescence excitation at 365 nm with blue emission. The excitation dependent emission CQDs are significant for fluorescence imaging of zebrafish at different wavelengths and different regions. As the discrete energy levels of PL spectra act as a proof of excitation dependent emission of PSE and GNSE CQDs.

The fibroblast cells were used in cytotoxicity assessment by direct MTT assay to evaluate the compatibility of cells with CQDs. The cell viability was investigated as a function of CQD concentrations (5, 25, 50, 75, 100 µL). Fig. 7 illustrates the CQDs with very low cytotoxicity, with over 95 % viability at a dosage of 100 µL for both PSE and GNSE CQDs following 90 % at a dosage of 50 and 75 µL for PSE and GNSE CQDs. Even at lower dosages (5 and 25 µL) of CQDs, the viability of the cells was greater than 65 %. The outcomes demonstrate that the CQDs have acceptable biocompatibility and are comparatively not toxic to fibroblast cells. The fluorescent CQDs’ minimal cytotoxicity attests to their applicability for bioimaging applications. As the CQDs are highly water soluble, they can easily disperse in biological media for more cell interaction and minimal toxicity. Moreover, the CQDs surface charge shows a significant effect on the cytotoxicity of the cells [44,45]. Thus, the positively and negatively charged PSE and GSNE CQDs interact accordingly with the cell cytoplasm of fibroblast cells.

The hemolysis examines the CQDs’ hemotoxicity. It was frequently used to inspect medications throughout the early stages of clinical development [46]. The hemolysis percentage observed for PSE and GNSE CQDs at low concentrations (1 mg/mL) is displayed in Fig. 8 , as 0.3 and 0.1 %, respectively. For PSE and GNSE CQDs at high concentrations (1 mg/mL) as hemolysis percentages of 1 and 1.8 % respectively. The ASTM E2524-08 standard states that biomaterials can tolerate less than 5 % hemolysis. Thus, we conclude that the biomass-derived CQDs are harmless to RBCs. Additionally, the specific surface charge of the CQDs interacts with the membrane of the RBCs, carefully premeditating the hemolysis activity [47]. Positively charged PSE CQDs (0.04 mV; Figure SI3b) lyse more quickly than negatively charged GNSE CQDs (−0.02 mV; Figure SI3a) at lower concentrations (1 mg). Higher concentrations of GNSE CQDs (5 mg) showed a direct correlation between the amount of hemolysis and CQD concentration. Therefore, the hemolytic effects are perilous for conceivable imaging purposes [48].

The tolerable antibacterial action against S. aureus and P. aeruginosa was verified by PSE and GNSE CQDs. (Figure SI2). PSE and GNSE CQDs show appreciable levels of response against the bacteria. S. aureus as gram-positive and P. aeruginosa as gram-negative with CQDs at different concentrations; at 100 µg/mL, all tested species showed complete growth inhibition [49]. Figure SI2(a-b), reveals the bacterial growth for static CQD concentration, among PSE and GNSE CQDs the PSE shows better inhibition than GNSE CQD by decreasing the spread of bacteria colonies. The oxidative stress, free radical formation, strong fluorescence emission, and structural damage to the cell membrane are caused by CQD antibacterial performance. These factors impact the function of bacterial cell wall synthetases and RNA cell impairment [50,51]. The antibacterial activity of GNSE CQD was decreased than PSE CQD due to their increased size and scattered morphology. This assessment provides visual insights into the intricate interplay, offering a detailed representation of the fluorescence signals emanating from the CQD-bacterial interaction. The fluorescent response serves as a valuable indicator, enabling the observation and characterization of the nature and extent of the interaction between CQDs and the bacterial strain. In specific, the CQDs produced intracellular singlet oxygen (1O2) during the antibacterial process [52]. Both P. aeruginosa and S. aureus suffered bacterial damage and abnormal morphologies as a result of these combined effects.

To confirm the protein’s instinctive activity and its biological activity, protein denaturation was performed. In the context of CQDs, the interaction between CQDs and proteins can influence the protein’s structure and function. Using a protein denaturation assay, the dose-dependent inhibition of BSA denaturation was cast to gauge the in vitro anti-inflammatory activity. Fig. 9 illustrates a notable inhibitory effect, reaching 98 % against BSA, for all concentrations (10, 30, and 50 mg) of PSE and GNSE CQDs. The observed protein denaturation is attributed to the structural deformation of inflammatory entities and the impact on the biological functions of the biomaterial (CQDs) [53]. Conspicuously, the BSA protein denaturation is an emblem of inflammation [54]. Consequently, the assessment indicates that PSE and GNSE CQDs inhibit the denaturation of BSA proteins, highlighting their potential as agents with anti-inflammatory properties and suggesting a potential application in the development of anti-inflammatory drugs.

Thus, the CQDs used to denaturize the BSA protein have anti-inflammatory assets. With the infringement of hydrogen bonds, and ionic interactions, the hydrophobic interaction with BSA becomes weakened when it interacts with the CQDs and thus the BSA was denaturized [55]. As a result, the BSA structure deviates from a fully folded macromolecule to an unstructured unfolded coil due to protein denaturation [56]. By denaturing BSA, CQDs may be able to reduce inflammation and open the door to new therapeutic strategies.

The gene efficiency of GNSE and PSE CQDs on the total RNA level was evaluated using real-time PCR. A comparison with the standard revealed upregulation in the PSE CQD and downregulation in the GNSE CQD. The upregulation observed in PSE CQDs indicates controlled proliferation during tissue formation. Conversely, the downregulation seen in GNSE CQDs suggests an increased number of growth factors/proteins secreted on the tissue surface, enhancing the metabolic activity of cells (Fig. 10 ). IGF-1 controls metabolic processes like protein synthesis, glycogen synthesis, and lipid metabolism. It also plays a substantial role in human growth and development. IGF-1 affects bone and muscle by encouraging myoblast or osteoblastic tissue differentiation and proliferation [57]. Collagen is the chain of proteins that helps for the formation of extracellular matrix (ECM), which supports and strengthens skin, cartilage, bone, and tendons. They positively influence cell attachment, migration, proliferation, and chondrogenic differentiation, particularly in connective tissues [58]. The alterations in various genes induced by CQDs present significant targets for understanding mechanisms and controlled drug delivery, with a substantial impact on the regenerative potential of chondrocytes. This suggests a potential avenue for utilizing CQDs in modulating gene expression for enhanced tissue regeneration and controlled therapeutic interventions.

To determine the subcellular structure and operation of organisms on the spatial and temporal scale [59], the fluorescence imaging of zebrafish was done using CQDs. Fluorescent bioimaging will be used as a non-invasive approach to visualize the zebrafish growth activities in the specified period [60]. The bioimaging assay was used to assess CQDs’ ability to be absorbed at various concentrations during the intracorporal development phase [61]. The zebrafish was washed after being exposed to CQDs for 24 h and then imaged. Fig. 11 (a-b), demonstrates the CQDs incubation upon zebrafish at various concentrations (10, 50, 75, and 100 µL). The CQDs show intense blue fluorescence (365 nm) at the yolk sac area of the zebrafish, in contrast, no fluorescence was seen for the control group. Bright fluorescence was seen when the CQD concentration increased. The PSE CQD exhibits greater absorption capability in the yolk sac and ocular area for all concentrations as compared to GNSE CQDs. Additionally, for the higher concentration, the yolk sac and eye region displayed the PSE CQDs absorption capacity. The performed uptake investigation specifics the potential for CQDs to bioaccumulate in the zebrafish larvae. The aforementioned results strongly endorse the capability of CQDs for bioimaging purposes. The findings unequivocally demonstrate that the synthesized CQDs can serve as a probe for in vivo bioimaging. Crucially, the cytoplasm can be stained by the CQDs owing to their effective capability to enter into the cell membrane. This suggests that CQDs have practical utility as imaging agents for live cell studies, particularly in the context of zebrafish larvae, showcasing their potential in biological and medical research applications.

In this work, the PSE and GNSE CQDs were fabricated using a cost-effective hydrothermal route. The CQDs were characterized by the means of physiochemical and biological properties. XRD data evidently demonstrates the amorphous natures with the presence of graphite, FTIR spectrum reveals momentous impact of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, Raman spectra indicate the formation of high graphitic band, the excitation and emission (Ex of 370 nm; Em of 456 nm and Ex of 309 nm; Em of 440) emanates fluorescence with the green and blue emission at the quantum yield of 9.5 and 9.0 % for PSE and GNSE CQDs respectively, which are crucial for imaging applications. The HRTEM images of GNSE CQD show scattered morphology and enlarged particle size (10–14 nm) than PSE CQDs (4.5–5 nm), due to GNS increased porosity and hydrophilicity. The in vitro assay of CQDs assesses the cell compatibility as 90 %, hemocompatibility as 0.3 and 0.1 %, and improved anti-inflammatory property was addressed by denaturation of BSA protein with 98 % inhibition using CQDs. GNSE and PSE CQDs have a stimulatory effect on the expression of IGF-1 and Col-1, shedding light on their potential roles in tissue regeneration and health. Furthermore, the in vivo bioimaging was tracked by CQDs in zebrafish larvae and visualized their uptake capacity, compatibility, behavior, and growth formation with bright fluorescence imaging at the yolk sac and eye regions of zebrafish. Biomass-derived CQDs are not more dangerous to cells and zebrafish than other CQDs made from chemicals. Furthermore, the eco-friendly biomass CQDs confirmed notable optical performance, biological compatibility, and photoluminescence, making them appropriate for biological imaging. As results suggest, the CQDs are a good probe for live imaging, promising theranostics reagents, and are anticipated to significantly advance wound-tracking applications.
N. Manjubaashini: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Validation. P. Bargavi: Formal analysis. S. Balakumar: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: NM and SB acknowledge the department of biotechnology, Government of India, for Research Associate fellowship, MHRD-RUSA [C3/RI&QI/RUSA2.0/Theme/Group-2/Award/2021/034, dt 03/02/ 2021] for funding and DST-FIST Funded X-Ray Diffractometer Facility, National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Guindy campus, Chennai [SR/FST/ET-1/2020/694 (C); dt 23rd June 2021].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115702.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green emitting carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) to probe endocytic pathways in cells; for tissue and in vivo bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Pankaj, S. Krupa, K. Krupa, D. Subhajit, K. Ashutosh, R. Rakesh, B. Dhiraj, Green emitting carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) to probe endocytic pathways in cells; for tissue and in vivo bioimaging, BioRxiv (2022) 10.1101/2022.04.23.489248.
